learning_objectives.text: Define substance use disorders and distinguish between use, abuse, and dependence
Describe global patterns of alcohol, tobacco, and illicit drug use across WHO regions
Identify major health consequences of substance use disorders
Recognize the epidemiology of prescription drug misuse globally
Explain the neurobiological basis of addiction and dependence
Interpret WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control provisions
Understand harm reduction principles and evidence base
Describe the relationship between substance use and blood-borne virus transmission
Apply evidence-based treatment approaches for different substance use disorders
Design culturally appropriate prevention programs for specific populations
Implement harm reduction interventions in diverse settings
Develop tobacco control policies using WHO MPOWER strategies
Analyze the effectiveness of different alcohol policy interventions
Compare criminalization versus public health approaches to drug policy
Evaluate the impact of tobacco taxation on consumption and health outcomes
Assess barriers to substance use treatment access across different healthcare systems
Integrate multiple prevention strategies into comprehensive substance use programs
Synthesize evidence on opioid substitution therapy effectiveness across different settings
Develop comprehensive tobacco control strategies combining policy, clinical, and community interventions
Create multi-sectoral approaches addressing substance use and related health issues
Evaluate the population-level impact of plain packaging tobacco policies
Judge the cost-effectiveness of different harm reduction interventions
Assess the human rights implications of different drug policy approaches
Critique the evidence base for universal versus selective prevention strategies

case_studies.title: Australia's Plain Packaging Tobacco Control Policy
intervention_type: Plain packaging legislation with graphic health warnings
key_lessons: Evidence generation for policy impact assessment
International diffusion of tobacco control innovations
discussion_points: How did Australia overcome tobacco industry legal challenges?
What role did research evidence play in policy development?
How can plain packaging be adapted for different regulatory environments?
What lessons apply to other tobacco control measures?

case_studies.title: Portugal's Drug Decriminalization and Treatment Expansion
intervention_type: Decriminalization of personal drug use with treatment expansion
key_lessons: Policy paradigm shifts from criminal to health approaches
discussion_points: What factors enabled Portugal's policy paradigm shift?
How did treatment system changes contribute to outcomes?
What role did political leadership play in sustaining reforms?
How applicable is this model to other country contexts?

case_studies.title: Scotland's Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol
intervention_type: Minimum unit pricing of £0.50 per unit of alcohol
key_lessons: Health inequities and targeted policy impacts
Political economy of alcohol policy implementation
discussion_points: Why did minimum unit pricing succeed when other price measures failed?
How did the policy address health inequities?
What role did industry opposition play in shaping implementation?
How can similar policies be adapted for different alcohol markets?

case_studies.title: Vietnam's Methadone Maintenance Therapy Scale-Up
intervention_type: Methadone maintenance therapy expansion from pilot to national scale
key_lessons: Health system strengthening for addiction treatment
Integration of harm reduction into existing health systems
Community engagement and peer support models
discussion_points: How did Vietnam overcome initial resistance to methadone programs?
What health system adaptations were required for scale-up?
How did community engagement contribute to program success?
What lessons apply to scaling treatment in other low-resource settings?

items.question: According to the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control, which intervention has the strongest evidence for reducing tobacco use?
options: a) Health education campaigns
b) Tobacco taxation and price increases
c) Smoke-free environment policies
d) Cessation support services
items.explanation: Tobacco taxation and price increases have the strongest evidence base for reducing tobacco consumption, particularly among youth and low-income populations, as demonstrated in systematic reviews and country-level implementation studies.

items.question: Which harm reduction intervention has the strongest evidence for preventing HIV transmission among people who inject drugs?
options: a) Needle and syringe exchange programs
b) Opioid substitution therapy
c) Drug consumption rooms
d) Both a and b have equally strong evidence
items.explanation: Both needle and syringe exchange programs and opioid substitution therapy have strong evidence for HIV prevention, with meta-analyses showing significant reductions in HIV transmission when implemented individually or in combination.

items.question: Population-level alcohol policy interventions with the strongest evidence include:
options: a) Education and awareness campaigns
b) Individual-level treatment programs
c) Alcohol taxation and availability restrictions
d) Workplace alcohol policies
items.explanation: Alcohol taxation and availability restrictions (including outlet density restrictions and hours of sale) have the strongest evidence for population-level impact, as demonstrated in systematic reviews and natural experiment studies.

items.question: The most effective approach to substance use prevention programming is:
options: a) Universal prevention for all populations
b) Selective prevention for high-risk groups
c) Indicated prevention for individuals showing early signs
d) A comprehensive approach combining all three strategies
items.explanation: Evidence supports comprehensive prevention approaches that combine universal, selective, and indicated prevention strategies, with different components targeting different population segments and risk levels.

items.question: Which statement best describes the evidence on drug policy approaches?
options: a) Criminalization approaches consistently reduce drug use and related harms
b) Public health approaches reduce drug-related harms but may increase use
c) Public health approaches reduce both drug use and drug-related harms
d) No approach has been shown to be effective
items.explanation: Evidence from Portugal, Switzerland, and other countries shows that public health approaches to drug policy can reduce both drug use and drug-related harms, including overdose deaths, infectious disease transmission, and crime.

items: Compare and contrast the evidence base for different tobacco control interventions using WHO MPOWER categories.
Analyze the key components of effective opioid substitution therapy programs in low-resource settings.
Evaluate the population-level impact of alcohol minimum unit pricing policies using Scotland's experience.
Describe the integration challenges for substance use treatment in primary healthcare systems.
Assess the role of community engagement in successful harm reduction program implementation.
